evidence_quotes.quote: end-of-support deadlines that have cut services keeping those games functioning, primarily due to having mandatory always-online requirements.

paragraphs.text: Greetings, Having played games for about 25 years now, inevitably, this means there are plenty of games in my childhood i can no longer enjoy because of end-of-support deadlines that have cut services keeping those games functioning, primarily due to having mandatory always-online requirements. To give a painfully clear example, a game used to exist called "The Cycle: Frontier". It was a wonderful game i and my friends used to play often until the developers decided that due to a lack of popularity and income, the servers would be shut down 3 months after the announcement. Today, that game is effectively dead. Noone can play it, all we have are memories. And if someone tries to remake the game, they will most likely receive a Cease and Desist letter, shutting that down aswell. The Digital Fairness Act aims to resolve situations like these so people who enjoy games and wish to relive the unforgettable experiences of their childhood or more recently can do so as they should expect to having paid with either money or time or both for said experiences. From what i understand, for a developer, it is not a herculean task to implement an offline-only feature for a game that at the very least preserves functionality within reason so the player can experience it's content without any patches/updates/ongoing support and would be notified of the game being in an unsupported state upon launching such a version. The only reason any group of people would be against this is to profit from it not because of any benefit for the players but to make sure that they are forced to purchase newer products to fill the void that the old ones have left. This is wrong and unjust. People who paid money or spent their own free time experiencing the passions of countless developers and artists should be able to revisit those memories if they wish to. If purchasing is not owning, then piracy is not stealing. Regards, Kevin Vardanian
